rows.v: The point where a player or object appears in the game world. 'Spawn point' is where you start or re-enter, and enemies 'spawn' into existence too.
The team rushed out of spawn the moment the round began.
From 'spawn' (to produce offspring, as fish do en masse). In games, characters and items 'spawn' into being at set points, often in waves.
To return to the game after dying, reappearing at a spawn point. Respawn rules — timers, locations, limits — shape much of competitive play.
After getting eliminated, she had to wait ten seconds to respawn and rejoin the fight.
From 're-' (again) + 'spawn'. The ability to respawn defines a game's stakes — instant respawns feel forgiving; permadeath makes every life precious.
Repetitive gameplay done to earn experience, currency, or loot. 'Grinding' is the long, often tedious path to leveling up or unlocking rewards.
He spent the weekend grinding the same dungeon to finally hit max level.
From 'grind' (laborious, repetitive work). The gameplay loop of repeating tasks for incremental rewards — sometimes satisfying, sometimes a chore.
Items, gear, and currency collected from defeated enemies, chests, or the environment. 'Loot' drives much of the reward and progression in games.
The boss dropped rare loot, including a legendary weapon she'd hunted for weeks.
From the Hindi 'lūṭ' (plunder, booty), absorbed into English via colonial India. In games it's the treasure and gear that reward exploration and combat.
The attention or hostility of enemies, especially AI-controlled ones. To 'pull aggro' is to make enemies target you — sometimes on purpose, sometimes disastrously.
The tank pulled aggro so the enemies attacked him instead of the fragile healer.
A clipping of 'aggravation' or 'aggression'. It names an enemy's targeting priority — managing who holds aggro is central to team-based combat.
The mandatory wait time before an ability can be used again after activating it. Cooldowns force players to time their powerful moves carefully.
She unleashed her ultimate, then had to survive ninety seconds of cooldown before using it again.
Descriptive: the ability must 'cool down' before reuse, like an overheated engine. Cooldowns balance powerful abilities by limiting how often they fire.
The invisible shape defining where a character or object can be hit. A shot landing within the hitbox counts; outside it misses, even if it looks close.
The complaint was that the hitbox was too big, registering hits that clearly should have missed.
Descriptive: the 'box' (or shape) that registers a 'hit'. This hidden geometry determines collisions and damage, and its size is a constant source of debate.
FPS (frames per second) — how many images the game displays each second. Higher frame rates mean smoother motion and a competitive edge.
He upgraded his rig to hit 240 frames per second for buttery-smooth, responsive aiming.
From film's 'frames'. A game's frame rate (confusingly also abbreviated FPS, like the genre) is prized by competitive players for smoothness and responsiveness.
A competitive game mode where wins and losses adjust your rank, matching you against similarly-skilled players. The ladder serious players climb.
She spent the season climbing ranked, grinding from Gold up to Diamond.
From 'rank' (a position in a hierarchy). Ranked play sorts players into tiers and divisions, providing the structured competitive ladder for the dedicated.
A rating system measuring player skill, used to match opponents fairly. 'MMR' (Matchmaking Rating) is the modern hidden version behind the scenes.
His Elo climbed steadily as he won, pairing him against tougher opponents each game.
Named for physicist Arpad Elo, who devised the system for chess. Adopted by video games as the math behind skill-based matchmaking and ranking.
A high-skilled player using a new or low-ranked account to play against weaker opponents. Often frowned upon for ruining matches for beginners.
The lobby suspected a smurf when one player dominated far above everyone else's level.
Originating in 'Warcraft', where two top players used the names 'Papa Smurf' and 'Smurfette' to hide their identity. The term stuck for any expert on an alt account.
The dominant strategies, characters, or tactics currently considered most effective. The 'meta' shapes how everyone plays — until it shifts.
After the patch, the meta shifted entirely, and yesterday's best character became useless.
Often explained as 'Most Effective Tactics Available', though it really comes from 'meta' (above/beyond) — the game-about-the-game of optimal strategy choices.
To win a high-pressure situation against the odds, often alone against multiple opponents. A 'clutch play' is the heroic, last-second victory.
Down to one player, he clutched the round with three straight eliminations.
From the sports sense of performing 'in the clutch' (a critical moment). A clutch is gaming's most celebrated feat — snatching victory from near-certain defeat.
To lose a game from a winning position through mistakes or poor decisions. To 'throw' is to snatch defeat from the jaws of victory.
They had it won, then threw the whole match with one greedy, reckless mistake.
From 'throw' a game (to deliberately lose), though in modern usage it usually means an accidental, painful collapse rather than intentional cheating.
'Good game' — said at a match's end as a sign of sportsmanship. 'GG' can be sincere, sarcastic ('GG EZ'), or a concession that the game is lost.
After the hard-fought match, both teams typed 'GG' in mutual respect.
An initialism for 'good game', a courtesy carried over from early online play. Its meaning bends with tone — gracious, dismissive, or a white-flag surrender.
A character built to absorb damage and protect teammates, drawing enemy fire while dealing less damage themselves. The team's frontline shield.
The tank charged in first, soaking up damage while the team followed behind.
From military 'tanks' — heavily armored and durable. The tank role trades offense for survivability, anchoring a team by absorbing punishment.
Damage Per Second — both a measure of damage output and the name for damage-dealing roles. 'The DPS' are the characters who do the killing.
The DPS focused the enemy healer while the tank held the line.
An acronym measuring 'damage per second'. It became shorthand for the damage-dealer role itself — the glass cannons who provide a team's offensive power.
A role focused on aiding teammates through healing, buffs, or utility rather than dealing damage. Healers keep the team alive; supports enable everyone else.
A great support, she kept the whole team topped up and shielded through every fight.
From 'support' (to assist). These selfless roles win games quietly, healing and empowering teammates rather than chasing kills and glory themselves.
Multiplayer Online Battle Arena — a genre where two teams of players each control a single hero, battling to destroy the enemy's base. Hugely popular in esports.
The MOBA's two teams fought lane by lane to push toward the enemy's base.
An acronym for the genre that grew from 'Defense of the Ancients'. MOBAs like League of Legends and Dota 2 became esports juggernauts.
First-Person Shooter — a genre played from the character's own viewpoint, centered on gun combat. Note: confusingly shares its initials with 'frames per second.'
He preferred the FPS genre, where fast reflexes and precise aim decided every duel.
An acronym for the genre seen through the character's own eyes ('first-person'). It shares its letters with the unrelated frame-rate measure — context tells them apart.
A genre where many players fight on a shrinking map until one player or team remains. Popularized by games like PUBG and Fortnite.
In the battle royale, a hundred players dropped in and the safe zone shrank until one was left.
Named after the Japanese film 'Battle Royale', about a last-one-standing fight. The shrinking-map, last-survivor formula became one of gaming's biggest crazes.
Massively Multiplayer Online — a genre where thousands of players share a persistent online world simultaneously. 'MMORPGs' add role-playing progression.
The MMO's world teemed with thousands of real players adventuring side by side.
An acronym for the 'massively multiplayer' experience. These vast, persistent shared worlds — World of Warcraft chief among them — host whole online societies.
To 'buff' is to strengthen something in a game update; to 'nerf' is to weaken it. Developers buff and nerf to keep the game balanced.
The patch buffed the underused character and nerfed the one everyone complained about.
'Buff' from making something stronger/'buffed up'; 'nerf' from the soft, harmless Nerf toys — to 'nerf' is to make a weapon as weak as a foam dart.
Crowd Control — abilities that limit enemies' actions, like stuns, slows, roots, or silences. CC controls fights by taking opponents out of the action.
She chained her crowd control to lock down the enemy team while her allies cleaned up.
An acronym for 'crowd control' — abilities that 'control' enemies by restricting their movement or actions. Landing CC at the right moment often decides team fights.
Damage over Time — an effect that deals damage gradually over a period, like poison or burning, rather than all at once. Steady, lingering harm.
His poison DoT ticked away at the boss's health long after the initial hit.
An acronym for 'damage over time'. Rather than a single burst, a DoT 'ticks' repeatedly, wearing an enemy down — and forcing them to react or cleanse it.
Area of Effect — an attack or ability that affects everything in a region rather than a single target. AoE clears groups of enemies efficiently.
He dropped an AoE spell on the clustered enemies, hitting them all at once.
An acronym for 'area of effect'. AoE abilities reward grouping enemies together (or punish players who clump up), shaping positioning in every fight.
When a chance-based effect triggers — like a weapon randomly dealing bonus damage. 'It proc'd' means the random effect activated.
Her sword had a chance to proc lightning, and it triggered at the perfect moment.
Believed to come from 'programmed random occurrence' or simply 'process'. It names the moment a probability-based effect fires, often at a lucky instant.
A weapon mechanic where shots register instantly along a line, with no travel time — if your aim is on target when you fire, it hits. Common in shooters.
The hitscan rifle hit instantly, so there was no need to lead moving targets.
A blend of 'hit' + 'scan' — the game instantly 'scans' for a target along the aim line. Contrasts with 'projectile' weapons whose shots travel and can be dodged.
Time to Kill — how long it takes to eliminate an opponent once you start landing hits. Low TTK games are fast and lethal; high TTK rewards sustained accuracy.
The game's low TTK meant a single burst of fire could drop an enemy instantly.
An acronym for 'time to kill'. A core measure of a shooter's feel — low TTK is punishing and twitchy, high TTK gives more room to react and recover.
A new or unskilled player. Sometimes neutral (a genuine beginner), often a mild insult for someone playing badly. Spelled 'noob', 'n00b', or 'newb.'
Don't worry about the mistakes — everyone's a noob when they start out.
A corruption of 'newbie' (a newcomer). 'Newb' tends to mean an innocent beginner, while 'noob' often implies someone unskilled who should know better.
Staying hidden in one spot to ambush opponents rather than moving around. An effective but widely disliked tactic, seen as cheap or cowardly.
He kept camping the same corner, picking off players who wandered past.
From 'camping' out in one place. The tactic of lurking in a fixed position to ambush passersby is effective yet scorned as unsporting by many players.
To quit a game abruptly in anger after losing or making a mistake. The dramatic, frustrated exit — sometimes with a slammed keyboard.
After the third loss in a row, he ragequit and shut the whole game down.
A compound of 'rage' + 'quit'. It names the all-too-human act of storming off mid-game in frustration — a meme as much as a behavior.
A 'one-shot' is an attack powerful enough to eliminate an opponent in a single hit. The dream of burst damage — and the bane of those on the receiving end.
Her sniper rifle could one-shot any enemy with a clean headshot.
Descriptive: the target is eliminated in 'one shot'. The ultimate burst payoff, one-shots reward precision but often spark balance complaints from victims.
'Pog' or 'poggers' expresses excitement or amazement at something awesome, especially in streaming chat. The reaction to an incredible play.
When the clutch landed, the whole chat erupted with 'Pog!'
From a Twitch emote ('PogChamp') showing a surprised face. 'Pog' became streaming culture's go-to exclamation of hype and astonishment at a great moment.
To 'carry' is to lead your team to victory through outstanding individual play, compensating for weaker teammates. The hard-carry wins games almost single-handedly.
She hard-carried the match, racking up most of the team's kills and dragging them to a win.
From 'carry' (to bear the load) — a dominant player 'carries' the team on their back. The term also names damage-focused roles expected to do exactly that.
